title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What is Life Insurance?
Life insurance is a legacy that you leave behind for your family, a financial shield that will protect your loved ones and help them to reach their goals in life irrespective of the circumstances. Life insurance is a policy that covers the risk of premature death. Moreover, many plans also have a maturity benefit that helps you create a corpus for your financial goals. Let’s understand the concept of life insurance and its salient aspects.
Life Insurance- Meaning
Life Insurance can be defined as a formal agreement between an insurance company and the policyholder. Under this agreement, the insurance company agrees to pay a mutually agreed amount of money to the beneficiary upon maturity of the plan or on the death of the insured in exchange for a premium. There are various types of life insurance policies, broadly terms insurance or pure protection plans and investment plans.
What are the benefits of life insurance plans?
A life insurance policy is an ultimate security that you and your family need. Apart from life cover, a life insurance policy provides various other benefits right, from the peace of mind, wealth creation, and financial stability to tax benefits. Following are the four primary benefits of life insurance plans:
Financial Security
The primary benefit of a life insurance policy is the financial security that it offers. Buying a life insurance policy with an adequate sum assured provides a financial cushion for you and your family members. This ensures that if you are not around, your family will have sufficient funds to take care of their financial needs. This provides you with financial security.
Long-term Savings
Life insurance policies enable long-term savings. Apart from life cover, it provides you with the opportunity to create wealth that can be used for fulfilling various life goals. With life insurance policies, you can make systematic savings and create a corpus to be used for various reasons, such as building a new home, your child's secondary education, or their marriage expenses.
Investment Options
Life insurance plans also provide investment options through unit-linked investment policies. These unit-linked investment policies are investment-oriented insurance plans that offer the dual benefit of life protection and market-linked return by investing the premiums of the plan in the capital market under a single financial product. These market-linked insurance policies provide significant gains to the policyholder by investing the premium in different classes of investment and delivering superior risk-adjusted returns to grow your corpus and beat inflation.
Tax Benefits
Life insurance also provides tax benefits. Life insurance policies offer two types of tax benefits. You can claim a tax deduction of up to 10% of the sum assured or the actual premium paid for the life insurance policy, whichever is less subject to a maximum of up to 1.5 lakhs per annum under section 80C of the Income Tax Act. Further, the death benefit, including the accumulated bonus received from the life insurance policy by the nominee, is fully exempt from tax. Maturity proceeds from the policy are also tax-free as per section 10 (10D) of the Income Tax Act, provided the premium paid for the policy in any of the years during the policy term does not exceed 10% of the actual sum assured. Hence, with life insurance, you can get both protection and savings on tax.
How does a Life Insurance Policy Work?
Under a life insurance policy, you choose the sum assured, the policy term, the premium payment term and frequency. Based on your age and these details, the premium is calculated. You pay the premium for the chosen term.
In exchange for the premium, the insurer covers you against the risk of premature death. In the case of death during the policy tenure, a death benefit is paid.
Alternatively, if you have selected a plan that has a maturity benefit, the benefit is paid when the plan matures.
Here are some examples to understand how life insurance works.
Examples
Mr Sharma buys a life insurance policy. It is a 20 years policy with the sum assured of Rs. 10 lakhs and an annual premium of Rs. 25,000 to be paid for 20 years.
Case 1: If Mr Sharma survives till the expiry of the policy term
Mr Sharma would receive a maturity benefit of Rs. 10 lakhs plus any accumulated bonus, if applicable
The life insurance policy will be terminated after payment of the maturity benefit
The amount of maturity proceeds of life insurance would depend on the type of life insurance policy brought
Case 2: If Mr Sharma dies in the 10thyear of the policy
Mr Sharma has paid a total premium of Rs 2.5 lakh for the policy
The nominee of Mr Sharma would receive Rs. 10 lakhs plus accumulated bonus, if applicable, on the year of death, i.e. on the 10th policy year
The life insurance policy will be terminated after payment of the death benefit
What are the Different Types of Life Insurance?
There are different types of life insurance plans available in the market. Each plan has its set of benefits and can be used for fulfilling different goals. Here’s a look at the different types of life insurance plans.
Term Life Insurance Plans
Term insurance is among the purest form of life insurance which provides life cover to the insured for a specified tenure. In the case of death during the selected tenure, a death benefit is paid to the nominee. Usually, term plans do not have a maturity benefit. So, if the insured survives the plan tenure, nothing is paid.
Unit-Linked Insurance Plans
Unit-Linked Insurance plans are investment-oriented plans in which the premiums of the policy are invested as per the risk appetite of the investor. There are various types of investment funds to choose from for investing in the premium. The policyholder can choose the fund option from debt, equity, liquid, balance, etc. ULIP plans are an effective long-term investment with a lock-in period of a minimum of 5 years. You also get flexible features like switching, premium redirection, top-ups and partial withdrawals.
Endowment Plan
Endowment plans are also known as savings-oriented life insurance plans. These plans offer both maturities as well as a death benefits. Under these plans, the death benefit is paid on the death of the life insured during the tenure of the policy to the nominee, and the maturity benefit is paid on the survival of the policyholder till the expiry of the tenure of the policy. Endowment plans also offers participating benefit in which accumulated periodic bonuses are paid on maturity or the death of the policyholder.
Money-back plans
Money-Back life insurance is a type of life insurance in which a specified portion of the sum assured in the plan is paid back to the insured at prescribed intervals in the form of survival benefits. Money-back life insurance helps you to attain short-term financial goals such as EMI payments, buying a car, etc. Moreover, a death benefit is also paid if the insured dies during the policy tenure.
Whole Life Insurance
Whole Life Plans are similar to term insurance plans with coverage to the insured till 99 or 100 years of age. Also known as unlimited term plans in which, life cover is provided to the insured till he/she attains 99 or 100 years of age and pays a death benefit in the event of death of the insured during the term of the plan. Moreover, nowadays, whole life plans also offer a maturity benefit if you survive till the end of the term.
Child Plan
A child insurance policy, as its name suggests, is specifically designed to provide financial security to your child. These plans help to build a corpus for a child’s future development and fund significant milestones of your child’s life, such as their higher education or marriage. Child plans might pay specified instalments following the major milestone of the life of your child or help you accumulate a corpus on maturity. Moreover, the future premiums of the plan are waived off in the event of the death of the parent to ensure the uninterrupted continuity of the plan.
Retirement Plan
Retirement plans  are designed specifically for your life after retirement. There are two variants of retirement plans: Deferred annuity plans and immediate annuity plans. Under deferred plans, you pay premiums and build up a retirement corpus. Under immediate annuity plans, you get immediate pay - outs right after buying the policy. The purpose of retirement plans is to enable an individual to live financially independently and worry-free even after retirement.
How much Life Insurance Do You Need?
The rule of thumb states that one should buy a life insurance cover of at least 10 or 20 times their annual salary to continue providing their family with a similar quality of lifestyle even in their absence. The thumb rules give you only an idea about the minimum cover you need, but that might not be enough. The key is to opt for an adequate sum assured in the plan to ensure that the future goals or lifestyles of your family do not get disrupted. The insurance cover is not solely dependent on your present income but dependent on the future value of the goals which you have set for your family.
As such, you need to assess your financial goals, income and expenses, assets and liabilities, number of dependents and other factors to assess how much life insurance coverage you need. (Interlinking with the article on how much coverage is needed)
What is Life Insurance Premium?
Life insurance premium refers to the amount paid to the life insurer in exchange for life insurance cover. It is the amount paid to get financial protection for your family in the event of untimely demise while the policy is in force. You pay the premium to the insurer, and in return, they provide coverage. Life Insurance premiums can be paid as regular payments, for a limited time or in a single instalment. The insurance companies also provide flexible premium payment modes which include monthly, half-yearly, quarterly and yearly.
Factors that determine your Life Cover
Your life insurance cover or sum assured depends on a lot of factors. These factors are as follows –
Your age
Younger your age, the higher the coverage that you need. This is because when you are young, the expected years to retirement are longer. Your family depends on the income that you are expected to earn till you retire. As such, death at a younger age causes a higher financial loss and to compensate for this loss, a higher sum assured is needed.
For instance, when you are 30, you are expected to work for the next 35 years. If you earn Rs.10 lakhs yearly, your family expects Rs.3.5 crores worth of income over the next 35 years. On the other hand, when you are 40, you are expected to work for the next 25 years, and at the same level of income, you are expected to earn Rs.2.5 crores. So, at 30, the sum assured needed higher than at 40.
Your income and expenses
Your income and expenses also determine the sum assured needed. If your income and expenses are higher, you need higher coverage and vice-versa.
Existing assets and liabilities
If you have existing liabilities, the coverage requirement goes up as you need funds to pay off the liabilities when you are not around. On the other hand, existing assets reduce the requirement for coverage since you have already made financial provisions for your goals.
Financial goals
The higher the number of financial goals you have higher would the coverage requirement. This is because you would need to create funds for your goals and the sum assured should be commensurate with the funds required.
Number of dependents
The number of dependents, who depend on you financially, directly impacts the coverage needed. More the number of dependents that you have higher would be the sum assured that would be needed to provide for their financial needs.
You should, thus, take these factors into consideration and then determine the coverage needed in your life insurance policy.
How to choose the ideal Life Insurance Policy?
Life Insurance is a necessary financial tool. To choose the ideal life insurance policy, firstly, you should be aware of your insurance requirements and secondly, you should assess the features and benefits of the different plans that are available in the market.
Follow these three steps to select the ideal life insurance policy for yourself and your family:
Get in touch with an Insurance Consultant/Provider
It is essential to get in touch with a knowledgeable and credible insurance provider or consultant when seeking to buy a life insurance policy. You may not be able to decide the type of insurance policy and how much insurance you need on your own. For this, you need the expertise of an insurance advisor as they can guide you to select the relevant policy as per your insurance needs. Moreover, the consultant can also help you compare the available plans and find the right one for your needs.
Evaluate the Life Insurance Cover
Next, assess the optimal coverage that you need. Ascertain your income, expenses, assets, liabilities, dependents and other factors to determine the coverage. You can also use life insurance calculators that help you determine the right sum assured based on your details.
Compare Life Insurance
Comparison is a very significant tool for selecting the ideal life insurance policy. Various insurance companies are offering different types of life insurance policies. As such, when you compare, you can find the relevant plan that offers maximum coverage benefits at the competitive premium rates.
How to claim Life Insurance after the Death of the Policyholder?
The death benefit under a life insurance policy is paid on the death of the insured during the term of the policy. The process of claiming life insurance after the death of the policyholder is very simple and quick. You have to inform the insurance company immediately after the death of the insured and fill out a claim form for making a claim. The claim form is to be submitted to the insurance company along with other relevant documents, which would then be verified by their executive only, after which the payout would be released. The payout may be made either in a lump sum or regular instalments as predetermined at the time of issuance of the policy. To claim life insurance as a nominee after the death of the policyholder, you have to follow the following three easy steps:
Death Certificate
Collect the death certificate of the insured issued by the local authority to start the claim process. The insurance provider may also ask for a certified copy of the death certificate, which should be kept handy to avoid delay in the claim process.
Contact the Insurer
Next, contact the insurance provider and inform them regarding the claim. The nominee should fill out the claim form and submit it to the insurance company when intimating about the claim.
Documentation
For the successful settlement of the claim, you would have to submit certain documents along with the claim form. Keep all the necessary documents handy before beginning the claim process. The documents required for life insurance death claims are as follows:
Original policy documents
Death certificate of the insured
ID proof of the nominee, such as an Aadhar card, voter ID, etc.
Bank account details of the nominee such as cancelled cheque or bank statement
Police FIR, panchnama, coroner’s report, etc. in the case of accidental deaths
Any other documents as required by the insurance company
How to Buy Life Insurance Online?
Now, buying a life insurance policy has been simplified, thanks to the online mode. You can buy a life insurance plan online in different ways. Have a look –
Through the insurer’s website
You can simply visit the website of the insurance company and check out online life insurance plans available. Pick a plan, fill out the online proposal form, upload your documents, pay the online premium and submit. The insurer would assess the form, verify the documents and issue the policy.
Through the insurer’s mobile app
Insurance companies have also launched their mobile applications. You can download the application on your Smartphone and buy online through the app.
Through an online marketplace
Online insurance marketplaces are there that allow you to compare and buy a suitable plan. You can visit such marketplaces, compare the plans, pick one and buy online.
Through online intermediaries
You can also visit the website of online insurance brokers or agents and buy life insurance through their websites.
Frequently Asked Questions
Is life insurance mandatory?
Life Insurance is not legally mandatory. However, it is a must-have for the financial protection that it provides. With a life insurance policy, you can secure against the risk of premature death giving your family financial security in your absence. Moreover, with the different types of policies available, you can plan a corpus for your financial goals.
Life insurance plans also give tax benefits and help you save in a disciplined manner. All these features make the plan a useful tool in your portfolio.
Do Life Insurance Plans offer any tax benefits?
Yes, life insurance plans offer tax benefits. Under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, you can claim a tax deduction of the premiums paid for the plan up to 10% of the sum assured, subject to a maximum of RS. 1.5 Lakhs per annum. Further, the death benefit received from the plan is tax-free in the hands of the nominee. Even the maturity benefit is tax-free under Section 10 (10D) if the premium is up to 10% of the sum assured. So, life insurance is preferred by individuals as it not only protects your loved ones financially but also is tax efficient.
How many types of life insurance plans are available in India?
There are various types of life insurance available to be brought in India. Some of these are:
Term Life Insurance Plans
Whole Life Insurance Plans
Unit Linked Insurance plans, also known as ULIPs
Child Plans
Savings or Endowment Plan
Retirement Plans, and
Money-Back Plans
What is the meaning of a Nominee?
A nominee is generally a family member, relative, or any other person to whom the death benefit is paid in the event of death of the insured during the term of the plan.
What are the premium payment modes available?
Life insurance premiums can be paid at regular intervals such as monthly, quarterly, half-yearly, and yearly. You can also make a one-time premium paid at the time of issuance of the plan. There is also a limited premium option wherein you pay premiums for a limited tenure.
What is the maximum number of nominees that I can add to the policy?
You can add more than one nominee to your life insurance policy. However, if you do so, you have to mention the proportion in which the benefit payout of the plan is paid to each nominee.
Is it possible to divide the payout among the appointed nominees?
Yes, you can divide the policy amount between nominees. In the case of more than one nominee in the plan, the payout gets equally divided amongst nominees. However, there are different terms and conditions under different life insurance policies, so it is better to first understand such details with your insurance provider to assess correct information regarding the addition of nominees.
What is the monthly premium payable for a life insurance policy?
Various factors affect the amount of premium to be paid for a life insurance policy. These factors, such as your age, health conditions, premium payment option selected, and sum assured in the plan, amongst others, determine the amount to be paid per month for life insurance as a premium. So, there is no fixed amount. The premium would depend on the plan you buy and the aforementioned parameters.
Is there any age criterion for buying life insurance?
The age limit varies from policy to policy. Life insurance plans can be purchased over the age of 18 years. However, the minimum age for the insured can start from as low as 3 months. There is also a maximum age up to which you can buy the policy. So, you need to check the plan details to find out the minimum and maximum age criterion.
What is the claim process if the policyholder dies?
If the policyholder dies, the claim is not initiated if the life insured is alive. However, if the policyholder and the insured are the same, the death claim would start. For the death claim, the nominee or the assigned would have to inform the insurance company. The documents required for the claim include:
Original Policy Documents
Application form for death claim
Original death certificate of the insured
Identify proof of the nominee
Bank mandates along with bank account passbook or cancelled cheque
Police FIR and other associated documents in the case of accidental death
Other documents are needed by the insurance company
What is the rule of thumb meaning for a life insurance policy?
The Rule of thumb for a life insurance policy signifies that a person should buy a life insurance policy with a minimum sum assured which is 10 to 12 times the annual income of the person. This means that if your current income is Rs. 8 Lakhs, you should buy a life insurance cover of Rs. 80 to 96 Lakhs.
Is it possible to get a cash benefit in the life insurance plan before death?
There is an option to surrender the plan wherein you get a cash value before the completion of tenure or before death. Surrender is available after the first few years of the policy, depending on the plan that you choose. Moreover, some plans, like term insurance, might not offer any surrender value at all.
There is also the availability of a policy loan under endowment and money-back plans. This helps you get a loan on your policy.
ULIPs also offer the feature of partial withdrawals, wherein you can get a cash benefit before death or maturity.
Define paid-up value in life insurance plans.
A paid-up value in an insurance policy is the value of the sum assured of the plan after you stop paying the premiums of the plan. The sum assured in the plan determined at the beginning of the policy is reduced if all the premiums for the plan are not paid. The sum assured that is proportionately reduced is called the paid-up value. The paid-up value is applicable on the lapse of the policy due to non-payment of premium. It is calculated as follows –
Paid-up value = {(number of premiums paid / number of premiums payable) * sum assured} + vested bonus
How can I revive a lapsed life insurance policy?
You can revive a lapsed insurance policy by paying the outstanding premiums. The insurance company may propose to submit a standard insurance revival form to reinstate the policy. Further, you need to pay the outstanding premium, penalty and interest, if any, along with the revival form. In some cases, a medical check-up might be needed for the revival of a lapsed insurance policy.
If the nominee dies before the insured, who gets the death benefit?
If the nominee dies before the insured, the nominee can be changed during the policy tenure. The death benefit would, thus, be paid to the changed nominee. On the other hand, if the nominee has not been changed, the policy benefits would be paid to the legal heirs of the insured.
